raw_consumer_statement:
Summary of Complaint : This is a formal complaint against American Express for : 1. Failing to apply a properly submitted payment to my account ; 2. Retaining and not returning a negotiable instrument issued in good faith ; 3. Misrepresenting the financial status of my account on monthly billing statements after receiving full payment ; and 4. Engaging in potentially unlawful securities practices by misreporting account balances and liabilities in violation of federal law. 

Case Background : On XX/XX/XXXX, a payment of {$25000.00} was sent to American Express XXXX XXXX Mail. XXXX confirmed the delivery on XX/XX/XXXX. This payment was issued in the form of a negotiable instrument intended to satisfy the outstanding balance. 

The following notices were subsequently submitted, including a Notice of Error, Notice of Default, and Notice of XXXX. American Express responded but did not apply the payment or return the original instrument. The account remains misreported as unpaid. 

This refusal to correct or update the account, despite acknowledging receipt of communication, is not only a violation of consumer protection law but also raises serious federal securities law concerns. 

XXXX XXXX Concern Misrepresentation of Accounts Under the Securities XXXX XXXX of XXXX, specifically Section 10 ( b ) and SEC Rule 10b-5, it is unlawful for any entity to make untrue statements of material fact or to omit material facts necessary to make statements not misleading in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. 

American Express operates in public financial markets and issues credit accounts which are securitized, traded, or leveraged as financial assets. By intentionally misrepresenting an account as unpaiddespite receipt of a lawful instrumentand potentially securitizing or reporting that account as a defaulted asset, American Express may be committing securities fraud.

In addition : The retention of the negotiable instrument without applying it or returning it may violate UCC 3-604, which defines discharge of obligation and prohibits mutilation or unauthorized retention.

Misreporting liabilities while engaging in structured finance can mislead investors and regulators regarding risk, value, and creditworthiness of securities backed by such receivables. 

American Express has been given multiple opportunities to resolve this matter in good faith. Instead, they have allowed the account to remain in dispute, potentially misrepresented the obligation internally or to third parties, and withheld or ignored evidence of lawful payment. Their continued misreporting not only violates consumer protection law but also may constitute securities fraud under federal statute.
